standard_description: Use commas in greetings and closings of letters.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Salutations, valedictions, dates, and addresses
khan_exercises.description: Commas are needed when starting or ending a letter/email, writing out dates, or writing out addresses.
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe

items.question_content:
**Address this letter by putting the commas in the right places.**

>Nero Wolfe 454 W. 35th Street New York New York

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Commas are needed when starting or ending a letter/email, writing out dates, or writing out addresses.

Addresses require a comma between each address part: Name, 123 Main Street, Anytown, USA.

The address is appropriately punctuated as follows:

>**Nero Wolfe, 454 W. 35th Street, New York, New York**

choices.content: Nero Wolfe$\redD{,}$ 454 W. 35th Street$\redD{,}$ New York$\redD{,}$ New York
Nero Wolfe$\redD{,}$ 454$\redD{,}$ W. 35th Street$\redD{,}$ New York New York
Nero Wolfe 454$\redD{,}$ W. 35th$\redD{,}$ Street New York$\redD{,}$ New York

items.question_content:
**Put the commas in the right place to start this letter or e-mail.**

>Dear Raj  
Thank you for the chocolates!


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Commas are needed when starting or ending a letter/email, writing out dates, or writing out addresses.

When addressing a letter, you put a comma after “Dear [Person’s name].” When ending a letter, you put a comma after the sign-off (“Love,” “Sincerely,” etc.)

The appropriately punctuation is as follows:

>**Dear Raj,  
Thank you for the chocolates!**

choices.content:
Dear Raj  
Thank you$\redD{,}$ for the chocolates!

Dear Raj$\redD{,}$  
Thank you for the chocolates!

Dear$\redD{,}$ Raj  
Thank you for the chocolates!

items.question_content:
**Punctuate this sentence by using a comma to separate place names.**

>He lives in Nome Alaska.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Commas are needed when starting or ending a letter/email, writing out dates, or writing out addresses.

When identifying a place, we need to put a comma between the city and the state or country: 

* *I am from Chicago, Illinois*.
* *It often rains in London, England*.

The appropriate punctuation is as follows:

>**He lives in Nome, Alaska.**

choices.content: He lives$\redD{,}$ in Nome Alaska.
He lives in$\redD{,}$ Nome$\redD{,}$ Alaska.
He lives in Nome$\redD{,}$ Alaska.

khan_exercises.title: Commas and introductory elements
khan_exercises.description: Basically, you can use commas at the beginning of sentences to set up an introduction.
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for commas in this sentence.**

Exhausted the basketball team boarded the shuttle home.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Introductory elements** come at the start of a sentence and provide useful descriptive information. We should *use a comma* to separate introductory elements from the rest of the sentence.

For example:

*  _Baring its teeth, the dog growled fiercely._ 
* _First, measure three cups of flour._

"Exhausted" is an introductory element, so we should separate it from the rest of the sentence with a comma:

>**Exhausted, the basketball team boarded the shuttle home.**

choices.content: Exhausted$\redD{,}$ the basketball team boarded the shuttle home.
Exhausted$\redD{,}$ the basketball team$\redD{,}$ boarded the shuttle home.
Exhausted the basketball team$\redD{,}$ boarded the shuttle home.

Basically, my work here is done.**

choices.content: Basically$\redD{,}$ my work here$\redD{,}$ is done.
Basically$\redD{,}$ my work here is done.
Basically$\redD{,}$ my work$\redD{,}$ here is done.
